text: Hello, I'm sharing an extended right colectomy. This is a patient who on an original colonoscopy was found to have four separate right-sided colon cancers. They were all very small polyps, between one and two centimeters, but all positive for well-differentiated adeno. all completely removed with negative margins and the most distal polyp was in the proximal transverse colon and a tattoo was placed distal to that there were no distal polyps and after genetic testing and consultation the patient opted for a colectomy as definitive management.
The surgeon ligates the ileocolic pedicle in an elderly patient, opting not to perform a complete mesocolic excision due to the small, well-differentiated tumors previously resected endoscopically.

The surgeon navigates over the head of the pancreas towards the right colic vessels, using a transverse orientation of the ports. The tattoo marking distal to the most distal cancer is visible, indicating the planned mesenteric transection from the middle colic to the margin.
The surgeon identifies and ligates the middle colic vein, visible adjacent to the middle colic artery, as part of the extended right colectomy procedure.

The surgeon identifies the middle colic artery and proceeds from the planned distal transection margin through the mesentery, using a vessel sealer to demonstrate the line of dissection and joining the area where the middle colic vein was previously taken.

The surgeon detaches portions of the mesentery, switching the camera from port three to two for better visualization. They proceed with the inferior dissection of the small bowel mesentery, using two right instruments and one left instrument.
The surgeon joins the medially dissected plane, utilizing the exposure gained from earlier port hopping to facilitate this step in the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon returns to the transverse colon and splits the omentum over the planned margin, preparing to resect the omentum along with the specimen.
The surgeon dissects through the gastrocolic omentum into the lesser sac, navigating around the lateral attachments. A sponge is visible at the site of previous medial dissection.

The surgeon navigates around the hepatic flexure, detaching the remaining attachments of the right colon and connecting to the previously completed inferior dissection, achieving full mobilization of the colon.
The surgeon uses a stapler at the distal transverse colon, followed by stapling the small bowel, with the camera positioned in arm two for optimal visualization.
The surgeon positions the stapler at the stapled end of the colon to create an isoparastaltic anastomosis, ensuring the stapler fits properly from this angle. The anastomosis is then sewn closed.

The final pathology proved that there was actually no residual carcinoma in the right colon. There were a couple of other small adenomas in the right colon and the margins were clean. And the patient did very well and happy with their decision. And I hope you enjoyed.
